segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens in a stark, white-walled corridor where a man in a dark suit and red tie, identified as James, stands before a black Restricted Stage Door. Visibly frustrated, James repeatedly attempts to open the door, trying the handle, inspecting the lock, and pressing buttons on the electronic keypad. He demands, "What's the problem?" to a calm, synthesized voice that responds, "Door's locked." James, growing exasperated, questions, "Why is the door locked?" The voice explains an "Issue with the passcode," prompting James to ask for it. As the voice dictates "4 5 6 5," James enters it, accompanied by keypad beeps, but declares, "No, it's not." The voice then suggests "4 5 6 6," which James also tries, again with keypad beeps, only to confirm, "No, it's not that." His frustration peaks as he accuses, "Are you just making up numbers?" to which the voice calmly replies, "Yes." James exclaims, "Oh my god," as the voice urgently states, "James, we have to start the show." James protests, "No, I'm not starting my final show in a stairwell," gesturing wildly. Despite the voice's insistence that "Network insists" and "Show has to start," James, overwhelmed and disbelieving, continues to struggle with the door, unable to gain entry to the area marked with 'DO NOT ENTER - RECORDING IN PROGRESS' and 'ALL CELL PHONES AND PAGERS MUST BE TURNED OFF BEFORE ENTERING STAGE' signs.

I'm coming, I'm coming, I'm coming," and turns to run down a Staircase marked 'RESTRICTED AREA,' his rapid footsteps audible. He quickly moves through a hallway with a neon 'The Late Late Show' sign, reaching another keypad door. A robotic voice announces, "The passcode is 1-1-1-1." James exclaims in disbelief, "1-1-1-1? You're joking!" After successfully entering, he walks through a dimly lit backstage area, continuing, "How we have done eight years here is an absolute miracle!" Backstage Crew Members clap and cheer enthusiastically as he passes. He expresses happiness that they are there, then announces his entire family, including his mom and dad, are present, prompting cheers and applause. The camera cuts to the Audience, focusing on a woman with short gray hair and a leopard-print scarf, who is smiling and clapping, and a bald man in glasses beside her, also smiling warmly. James asks his parents if they're "holding it together," to which the woman, his mother, responds with a warm, slightly emotional voice, recalling how he asked her for a kiss before the show, causing her to become emotional and dabbing her eyes with a tissue. James then playfully teases her about her "botox," joking that he "didn't even know you could move your face that much" and that it was "the most of all the botox," leading to more audience laughter and gasps. He continues, recounting his mother's concern about being able to "come out here" and her explanation about needing "a certain collagen for my lips that I can't get at home," again met with laughter. Throughout, James maintains broad gestures and eye contact, fostering a lively and heartfelt interaction. The segment concludes with him transitioning to the show's "sacred duty to give you, the American people, the biggest, most important news stories," all set against the show’s warm studio ambiance and stylized cityscape background with purple and orange hues.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Man in Suit, an enthusiastic speaker in a dark suit and red tie, seated at his Desk Setup. He continues, stating that "pet birds are better with technology than your mom and dad," as the audience laughs and applauds. The scene cuts to a close-up of a young girl holding a tablet with a blue protective case, showing a white parrot on screen, which appears to be interacting with the device. A graphic overlay appears, displaying an Instagram profile named 'Parrot' with a photo of the same white parrot wearing a red bra, under the handle 'OnlyFeathers'. The Man in Suit reacts with amusement, laughing and gesturing toward the screen as upbeat, jazzy/funk music begins to play. The scene briefly shifts to the Live Band, featuring a keyboardist with an afro and sunglasses playing a Nord Lead keyboard, and a woman in a white blouse seated beside him. The Man in Suit resumes his commentary over the music, making a peace sign and expressing gratitude to the audience for watching their show "night after night" on CBS, mentioning "Dan, Stephanie, William," which prompts more laughter. He then asks, "Ian, are you ready? You're ready for one last rise?" segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Suit, identified as James, seated at a desk setup, speaking expressively and gesturing, even making a peace sign, as he discusses the show's early timing. The scene cuts to the Man in Dark Jacket at a Laptop Desk, also gesturing with a peace sign, who continues the conversation about someone relying on their show for news and wanting to send a quick message. A split-screen then appears, showing James in a small inset with his head down, while the main screen displays President Joe Biden speaking in front of American and presidential flags. Biden's distinct, deeper voice congratulates James on an "incredible run" of "1200 shows in eight years," humorously adding, "after a day you spent working for me at the White House." The scene shifts to a staged Oval Office where James, now in a black jacket, presents a bouquet to President Biden, who gestures while Biden's voice continues, describing the gift as "both a snack and a gift," and jokingly remarking, "I'm surprised you lasted eight years in any job. Who is that guy?" Biden then thanks James, Reggie, and the entire team for the joy they've brought to homes across America. As orchestral, dramatic music begins, the video cuts to a live stage performance where James stands beside a Musician with an Afro and Sunglasses playing a Nord Lead keyboard, with the audience visible. A wide shot follows, showing the entire cast and crew of 'The Late Late Show' posing on stage, still accompanied by the music. The video returns to a split-screen: James is in the inset, reacting with his hand to his mouth, while the main screen shows Men in Sailor Outfits dancing joyfully in the rain on a New York City street, with a 'Miss Saigon' sign visible, all set to the continuing orchestral score. Next, James is seen laughing in a car with a Man in Blue Superhero Costume. The final split-screen shows James in the inset, hand to mouth, as President Biden speaks again, this time with a Woman in a Silver Sequined Dress and red bow tie beside him, both gesturing. Biden's voice returns to deliver a "special thanks for never asking me to sing in a car," followed by laughter. The video concludes with a close-up of James in the car, mouth open as if shouting or singing, with a Woman in a Red and White Patterned Dress beside him, both with hands raised in excitement amidst the laughter.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a staged, comedic scene in a mock Oval Office, where the Man in Suit (wearing a black jacket, dark pants, and white sneakers) sits beside President Joe Biden, who is dressed in a formal dark suit. Both wear sunglasses, and Biden is heard speaking reflectively, saying, "can't say worth the damn," and later, "We'll miss you, pal. I can't wait to see what you do next," as he sips from a cup. The scene quickly cuts to President Biden in a formal address, standing in front of American and presidential flags, speaking with expressive hand gestures. The video then transitions to the set of 'The Late Late Show with James Corden,' where the Man in Suit (now in a dark suit and red tie, identified as James Corden by his voice) is seated at the Desk Setup. He initially rests his head on the desk in mock exhaustion before lifting it to speak with exaggerated enthusiasm, gesturing broadly, pointing, and making a peace sign, engaging with the audience. Loud, sustained audience applause and cheers erupt as he speaks. Corden, with a distinct comedic British accent, exclaims, "That is amazing," and humorously questions, "Wait, have I died?" prompting audience laughter. Another male voice is heard mentioning a segment from "Trump," to which Corden comically reacts, "Oh, no, don't do this. No. Oh, no, I can't. What?" The camera cuts to the Live Band, featuring a Musician with Afro and Sunglasses playing a Nord Lead keyboard and a woman in a white blouse beside him, both smiling and clapping. Corden continues his energetic monologue, laughing and gesturing, announcing, "After the break, we got a big surprise for you. Don't go anywhere. This is the last time we're ever gonna do this. Come on back everybody!" as the audience applauds. The scene briefly shows a Man in Dark Jacket at Laptop Desk also making a peace sign, before returning to Corden, who is laughing heartily. The video ends with a wide shot of the Stage, showing the audience clapping and the band performing upbeat, lively music, with the show’s logo visible on the backdrop.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a live band performing energetic rock music on a stage with a checkered floor and blue-lit panels, as a crowd cheers and claps. The scene transitions to the Man in Suit, wearing a dark suit and red tie, seated at the Desk Setup. He speaks expressively to the camera with hand gestures, welcoming back the audience and reflecting on the emotional end of the show, humorously noting he might finally get a good night's sleep, which elicits laughter from the audience. The setting then shifts to a dimly lit bedroom where the Man in Suit, now in black polka-dot pajamas, is asleep in bed. He is awakened by a digital alarm sound, lets out a loud yawn, and then exclaims "Oh my god!" in surprise. A Man in Blue Shirt sits beside him, clasping his hands and speaking earnestly, greeting him as "Hello James." The Man in Suit, surprised, asks "What are you doing here?" to which the Man in Blue Shirt calmly replies he's watching him sleep, prompting the Man in Suit to point out "It's 3 o'clock in the morning!" The Man in Blue Shirt then states he needs to talk to him. The Man in Suit, looking increasingly bewildered, asks "About what?" The Man in Blue Shirt explains they are there to tell him he "can't just leave a talk show." As the Man in Suit questions "We?", a third man, dressed in a black suit and tie, appears seated in a chair beside the bed. This Man in Black Suit and Tie then addresses the Man in Suit as "James," explaining that hosting a network late-night talk show makes him part of a very exclusive club, and if they're going to let him leave, they need to know he'll keep their "sacred..." before the audio cuts off. The lighting remains soft and intimate throughout the bedroom scenes, contrasting with the bright stage and studio lighting earlier.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Suit, dressed in a dark suit and red tie, seated at a black Yamaha grand piano on a dimly lit stage. He sings emotionally into a microphone, his clear voice delivering the lyrics "no longer ask people questions that aren't the least bit apropos," accompanied by the piano and a full band. Warm stage lighting casts soft glows, reflecting on the piano's surface. The camera cuts to a wider shot, revealing the Live Band behind him, including a Musician with Afro and Sunglasses playing a Nord Lead keyboard, a Woman in White Blouse playing bass, and a Keyboardist with Gray Hair at a KORG Kronos, all providing instrumental accompaniment. The Choir, mostly women, stands silhouetted behind the piano, singing in harmony. The Man in Suit then speaks, asking "What is the nature of time?" which elicits laughter from the Audience, before he adds, "Thanks, buddy, helpful as always." The scene transitions to a wide view of the Stage, showing him at center, illuminated by spotlights, with the attentive Audience seated in rows. He continues to sing, "So goodnight. Thanks for watching. That's our show," as the music swells. A montage of behind-the-scenes moments follows, showing three people laughing on a couch with scripts, a group posing with a wedding cake, a man in a studio with headphones, a split-screen of smiling crew members, a man in a control room, and two women holding 'Late Late Prom King' and 'Sing On Swing' signs, all while the Man in Suit's expressive singing continues, detailing the crew's work: "That's our show, the producers, directors, designers, the ones who set the lights and answer phones and do the edit, writing sketches, working cameras, building graphics, making costumes, booking talent, mixing sound and never getting any credit. Our show was a creation of the love and dedication shared by everyone here in the crew." The video returns to the stage, showing the Man in Suit in close-up, then a wide shot of the stage with the Audience clapping and cheering. He sings reflectively, "I'm grateful for the eight years I spent with them, and the eight years I spent here with you," as the music becomes more tender. The final frames show the Man in Suit again in close-up, then a wide view of the stage with the Audience and the full band, all under dramatic lighting, creating an intimate and powerful performance atmosphere as he concludes, "As the end of..." and the music gently fades.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a dimly lit stage, bathed in warm, dramatic spotlights cutting through a hazy atmosphere. At the center, a black Yamaha grand piano is illuminated from below, where a Man in Suit, dressed in a dark suit and red tie, is seated. He sings into a microphone with deep emotional expression, his eyes often closed, delivering a melancholic and reflective song in a clear male voice. His lyrics speak of a show ending and cherished memories, beginning with, “The show fast approaches. I gaze at the studio floor. We filled it with thousands of moments of joy. And now there won't be any more.” As the camera slowly zooms in, focusing on his passionate delivery, a Choir of mostly women behind him are silhouetted, providing harmonious backing vocals. The audience, visible in shadow, watches attentively. The stage floor reflects the lights, enhancing the intimate, theatrical ambiance. The singer continues, “That each of those thousands of moments of joy is the one I'll remember the most,” and later, “No more shows to be showing. It's time I was going. It's time. Thanks for watching. That's our show.” The camera tightens on his face, his reflection visible in the glossy piano, as the warm, focused lighting emphasizes the emotional weight of his farewell performance, creating an atmosphere of quiet reverence and artistic expression.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with the Man in Suit, dressed in a dark suit and red tie, seated at a wooden desk on a dimly lit stage. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, delivering a clear, somewhat emotional farewell, stating, "actually a time everybody, I will miss you. Good night." Immediately following his words, upbeat, celebratory rock/pop music begins to play, accompanied by strong, sustained applause and enthusiastic cheering from the audience. The scene transitions to a wide shot of the stage, where the Man in Suit walks through the cheering audience, who are clapping and smiling enthusiastically. He moves toward the edge of the stage, waving to the crowd, and then steps behind a heavy dark curtain, disappearing from view, all while the music and applause continue. The camera pans across the audience, capturing their continued excitement. A female vocalist joins the music, singing discernible lyrics like "so good to be loved" and "so good to be alive." On-screen text appears, crediting the executive producers: Bob Weinstein, James Conran, and Glenn F. Clements, followed by production details: 'PRODUCED AND RECORDED AT: TELEVISION CITY STUDIOS, HOLLYWOOD, CALIFORNIA.' The video concludes with the Fulwell 73 Productions logo, followed by the CBS logo and copyright notice: '© 2023 CBS BROADCASTING INC. ALL RIGHTS RESERVED.' The atmosphere is celebratory and professional, with warm stage lighting and a sense of closure as the show ends, underscored by the persistent music and applause.
